content.text: Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout model — it arranges items along a single axis (either horizontal or vertical). It excels at distributing space among items in a container, aligning items, and building UI components like toolbars, navigation bars, card grids, and centered layouts. If you need two-dimensional layout, use CSS Grid instead.

attrs.alt: Flexbox container and item properties with justify-content and align-items visual diagrams

content.text: The Two Roles: Container and Item

Flexbox divides responsibility between the container (the element with display: flex) and its direct children (the flex items). Container properties control the flow, spacing, and alignment system. Item properties let individual children opt out of or override those defaults.
Only direct children of a flex container become flex items. Grandchildren and deeper descendants are not affected.

Every flex container has two axes. The main axis runs in the direction of flex-direction. The cross axis is perpendicular. Understanding which properties operate on which axis prevents most Flexbox confusion:
Alignment of items perpendicular to flex-direction
Spacing between rows/columns when wrapping is on
Items packed to the start of the main axis
Items centered on the main axis
First/last items flush to edges; equal gap between items
Equal space on each side of every item (halved at edges)
Equal space between every item AND the edges
Items stretch to fill the container's cross-axis size
Items aligned to the start of the cross axis (top for row)
Items aligned to the end of the cross axis (bottom for row)
Items centered on the cross axis
Items aligned so their text baselines line up
By default, flex items are forced onto a single line even if they overflow their container. flex-wrap: wrap allows items to flow onto the next line when they run out of space:
The flex Shorthand — grow, shrink, basis

The flex shorthand on items sets three properties at once: flex-grow (how much the item expands to fill free space), flex-shrink (how much it contracts when space is tight), and flex-basis (its initial size before growing or shrinking).

/* flex: <flex-grow> <flex-shrink> <flex-basis> */

.item { flex: 1; }          /* 1 1 0  — equal-width columns */
.item { flex: auto; }       /* 1 1 auto — sized by content, then flexible */
.item { flex: none; }       /* 0 0 auto — rigid, no grow/shrink */
.item { flex: 0 0 200px; }  /* fixed 200px, no grow/shrink */
Equal sizing — all items share all available space
Natural size first, then distribute remaining space
Sized to content, never grows or shrinks
Fixed size — a column that never changes
gap (formerly grid-gap) sets consistent spacing between flex items without affecting the outer edges. It replaces the older pattern of using margins with :last-child overrides:

Flex items have a default minimum size of min-width: auto (or min-height: auto for column direction). This means a flex item will not shrink below its content width, which can break layouts with text overflow or nested scrollable areas.
If a flex item with flex: 1 is not shrinking as expected, add min-width: 0. This is especially common with text truncation and nested overflow: auto containers.

The most common Angular pattern is setting display: flex on :host so the component element participates in the parent's flex layout, and then laying out internal elements with flex on the component's root element:
Angular CDK layout utilities (BreakpointObserver) can complement flex-wrap for adapting layouts at breakpoints, but most responsive flex patterns can be achieved with CSS alone.
Laying out items along one axis (toolbar, nav, card row)
Creating a two-dimensional layout (page layout, data table)
Unknown number of items that should wrap naturally
You need precise control over both rows and columns simultaneously
The item sizes should be determined by their content
Items should snap to a defined grid structure
You need auto-margins to push one item to the opposite side
You need items to span multiple rows or columns
Forgetting min-width: 0 — flex items refuse to shrink below content width by default
Using flex on grandchildren — only direct children of display: flex become flex items
justify-content: center on a single-item container — works, but align-items: center is often the missing piece
Mixing flex: 1 with flex: 0 0 200px in the same row without accounting for gap — gap reduces available space
align-items vs align-content confusion — align-items for single-line, align-content for wrapped multi-line
Angular :host not block — adding display: flex on a child of a flex container that itself has no display set (it defaults to inline)

Lines stretch to fill the container

Stack on Mobile, Row on Desktop
When flex layout behaves unexpectedly, work through this checklist:
Confirm `display: flex` is on the container
, not an item. Use DevTools Flex badge.
— items sized by content may not shrink/grow as expected.
— a child that is itself a flex container may clip its contents.
on a flex container can mask layout issues.
does not apply outside the container; use
Temporarily applying an outline to all flex children immediately reveals which items are shrinking, overflowing, or collapsing unexpectedly.
. Screen readers and keyboard navigation follow the DOM, so visual and accessible order can diverge:
If content order matters for comprehension (not just aesthetics), reorder the HTML itself rather than using CSS order manipulation.
can be used in exceptional cases where visual order must differ from DOM order, but it requires explicit maintenance and is generally fragile — restructure the DOM first.

Flexbox arranges direct children along one axis. Container properties control the layout system: flex-direction sets the main axis, justify-content distributes space along it, align-items aligns items on the cross axis, flex-wrap enables multi-line layout, and gap sets consistent spacing. Item properties let children adjust individually: flex (grow shrink basis) controls sizing, align-self overrides alignment, and order changes visual position. The flex: 1 shorthand creates equal-width columns; flex: none makes an item rigid. Add min-width: 0 when a flex item must shrink below its content width. In Angular, set display: flex on :host to participate in parent flex containers, and on internal wrappers to compose component layouts.
